notes: Our knowledge of holy sanctuaries which existed before the day of Moses is slight. We do know that as soon as Moses led Israel out of Egyptian bondage detailed instructions were received to build and use a portable temple or tabernacle, not for general assembly and meetings, but for the performance of holy ordinances. It appears that initially there was in ancient Israel a provisional tabernacle (Ex. 33:7-11); that thereafter the people donated of their riches and the tabernacle of the congregation was built (Ex., chapters 25 to 31 and 35 to 40); that this tabernacle was set up at various places in Israel after they entered their promised land (Josh. 18:1-3; Judges 18:31; 21:2; 1 Sam. 1:3, 24; 4; 7:1-2; 21:1-6; 1 Chron. 21:28-30; 2 Chron. 1:3-6); that while the tabernacle was at Gibeon, David erected another

quotes.text: The passage about the Tent of Meeting and its being set up outside the camp (vv. 7-11) is somewhat parenthetical, but it is of great importance in the context of chapters 32-34. God had just announced that he would not go in the midst of the people (vv. 3, 5). It is not until 34:9-10 that God finally agreed to come back in the midst of his people. Therefore, between these two points in the narrative God is conceived of as being outside the community. Now we learn elsewhere that God's presence was connected with the location of the sanctuary. In the sanctuary prescriptions God said that he would dwell in the midst of the Israelites (25:8; 29:45-46; cf. Num. 5:3); Num. 2:2, 17 place the location of the Tent in the middle of the camp (see also the location of the sanctuary and its appurtenances in the wilderness travels in Numbers 10:13-28). Therefore, the story in Ex. 33:7-11 of the Tent of Meeting being set up outside the camp makes perfect sense here. Since God refused to dwell among the people, the Tent needed to be set up outside the camp. Later, after God's forgiveness was obtained and the covenant was renewed, it was set up inside the camp (Numbers 2:2).
quotes.speaker: David Wright in Studies in Scripture
quotes.attr: David Wright in Studies in Scripture, Vol. 3: Genesis to 2 Samuel, 137

notes: Moses stands preeminent above all Israel's prophets. "With him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches," the Lord said, "and the similitude of the Lord shall he behold." (Num. 12:8.) Hence we read: "And the Lord spake unto Moses face to face, as a man speaketh unto his friend." (Ex. 33:11.) And: "Moses stood in the presence of God, and talked with him face to face." (Moses
face to face Many times these revelations are called "Theophanies". Theophany is Greek for "divine appearance," or "appearance of God."

quotes.text: The doctrine that man is created in the image of God was also lost in the apostasy. The vision given to Joseph Smith restored the true doctrine in relation to this question. It is just as strange that man, in his spiritual darkness, would change this glorious doctrine and in these later times substitute for it the abominable doctrine that man has ascended through countless ages from lower forms of life, as it is that they could make of the Father and the Son and the Holy Ghost a God to be worshipped that is without substance, immaterial and therefore non-existent. Here are a few references showing the error of the teaching of the so-called Christian world, all creeds and denominations, on this point, and proving that God has both body, parts and passions: Gen. 1:26. 5:1 and 9:6. James 3:8-9. Exodus 33:9-23. Numbers 12:7-8. Ex. 20:5. Deut. 4:24.) The Savior being God-the Son, and a member of the Godhead-as believed by most denominations, and since he had a body, and that body was tangible after his resurrection; then we have definite proof that God has a body of flesh and bones as tangible as man's. His disciples handled him after his resurrection; he ate in their presence (Luke 24:38-44). He has promised to return with that same body and reveal it, even showing the prints of nails in his hands to the unbelieving Jews (Zech. 12:10. 13:6. 14:4. Compare D. and C. 45:49-53). Since there are innumerable passages proving this doctrine, have members of the class present as many as they can.
quotes.speaker: Joseph Fielding Smith
quotes.attr: Joseph Fielding Smith, Church History and Modern Revelation, 4 vols., 1:, p.11

quotes.text: It is in chapter 12 that we gain a keener insight into Moses' character, an insight obtained first from a verse presumably written by Moses himself: "Now the man Moses was very meek, above all the men which were upon the face of the earth" (Num. 12:3). The Lord descended in a cloud, summoned Miriam and Aaron before him, and continued to expand our understanding of Moses' greatness and unusual prophetic stature: "If there be a prophet among you, I the Lord will make myself known unto him in a vision, and will speak unto him in a dream" (Num. 12:6). One would suppose that such divine manifestations (visions or dreams) on a regular basis would be commendable and laudatory, even for prophets. But Moses was unusual: "With him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches; and the similitude of the Lord shall he behold" (Num. 12:8). All revelation is marvelous, but some is more direct than others. In some cases "the Spirit speaketh expressly" (1 Tim. 4:1), implying that sometimes it does not speak quite so clearly. Joseph Smith spoke of the "still small voice, which whispereth through and pierceth all things, and often times it maketh my bones to quake while it maketh manifest" (D&C 85:6). As it is with revelation, so it is with prophets: some prophets enjoy a greater intimacy with the Lord than others. Moses spoke with the Lord face to face (cf. Moses 1:2; Ex. 33:11).
quotes.attr: Robert L. Millet in Kent P. Jackson and Robert L. Millet, eds., Studies in Scripture, Vol. 3: Genesis to 2 Samuel, p.189-190

quotes.text: Moses was the head of a dispensation and thus a prophets' prophet. Of him the Lord Jehovah explained to Aaron and Miriam: "If there be a prophet among you, I the Lord will make myself known unto him in a vision, and will speak unto him in a dream. My servant Moses is not so, who is faithful in all mine house. With him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches; and the similitude of the Lord shall he behold" (Numbers 12:6-8; see also Exodus 33:11). But the above verse indicates that Moses-like all prophets, and, for that matter, all men and women-walked most often and most surely by the kindly light and whisperings of the Spirit.
quotes.speaker: Robert L. Millet
quotes.source: Selected Writings of Robert L. Millet: Gospel Scholars Series
quotes.attr: Robert L. Millet, Selected Writings of Robert L. Millet: Gospel Scholars Series, p.429

quotes.text: Moses is saying to Jehovah: You want me to bring up this people to the promised land, yet you told me you won't go with me. You claim to know me and you know my name. That means you know me. You know that I can't do it alone. Then you also say that I have found grace in thy sight. Now Moses starts to define his own form of GRACE FROM THE LORD. 13. Now therefore, I pray thee, if I have found grace in thy sight, shew me now thy way, that I may know thee, that I may find grace in thy sight: and consider that this nation is thy people. THE FIRST TIME THAT GRACE IS DEFINED IN THE SCRIPTURES IS HERE. IF YOU HAVE GRACE, THEN GOD SHOWS YOU HIS WAY. Another way of saying your "way" is your NAME. IN OTHER WORDS, MOSES WANTS TO KNOW THE NAME OF GOD. What Moses is asking is for Jehovah to reciprocate grace...you know my name, teach me your name. You know me. Reveal yourself to me, so I can know you. That is true grace, the revelation of GOD'S WAYS TO HIS PEOPLE. IF IT IS REAL GRACE, THEN THIS PEOPLE WILL LEARN YOUR NAME, AND LEARN YOUR WAY, AND BECOME YOUR PEOPLE. [quotes Exodus 33:14-16] If you are willing to go with us, that is what will make us different from all the people on the earth. We will be "peculiar people" or private treasure, and become "my people." It can only happen, God, if you go with us. So now GRACE is defined as the LORD showing Moses his way, and going with them, and having HIS PRESENCE IN THEIR MIDST. It is that Presence that separates them from all the other people on the earth. [quotes Exodus 33:17-19] Now, this was all done in a little tent down here at the base of Mt. Sinai.
quotes.speaker: Robert Norman
quotes.attr: Robert Norman, 1989 Old Testament Class Notes

quotes.text: Because mercy is an attribute of Deity (Ex. 33:19; Rom. 9:15-18; 2 Cor. 1:3; Eph. 2:4; 1 Pet. 1:3), men are thereby enabled to have faith in him unto life and salvation. They must have "the idea of the existence of the attribute of mercy in the Deity," the Prophet says, "in order to exercise faith in him for life and salvation; for without the idea of the existence of this attribute in the Deity, the spirits of the saints would faint in the midst of the tribulations, afflictions, and persecutions which they have to endure for righteousness' sake. But when the idea of the existence of this attribute is once established in the mind it gives life and energy to the spirits of the saints, believing that the mercy of God will be poured out upon them in the midst of their afflictions, and that he will compassionate them in their sufferings, and that the mercy of God will lay hold of them and secure them in the arms of his love, so that they will receive a full reward for all their suffering . . . . And as mercy is . . . an attribute of the Deity, his saints can have confidence that; it will be exercised towards them, and through the exercise of that; attribute towards them comfort and consolation will be administered unto them abundantly, amid all their afflictions and tribulations." (Lectures on Faith, pp. 46-47.)
quotes.speaker: Bruce R. Mc Conkie
quotes.attr: Bruce R. Mc Conkie, Mormon Doctrine, 2d ed., p.483

notes: There are several statements in the KJV in which there are direct statements and inferences that mortal man cannot see God and live. The most prominent of these are in KJV Exodus 33:20, John 1:18, 1 John 4:12, and 1 Timothy 6:15-16. These passages stand in contradiction to other KJV passages wherein it is declared that Moses and seventy elders saw God (Exodus 24:9-10) or that Moses saw God "face to face" (Ex. 33:11) or that God was seen by Isaiah (Isaiah 6:1) or Abraham (Genesis 18:1) or Jacob (Genesis 32:30) and a host of others. The JST brings order out of these contradictions by inserting certain concepts that are missing in the KJV. (Robert J. Matthews, "Plain and Precious Things Restored," Ensign, July 1982, 19).

quotes.text: Having been assured of a private manifestation of the Divine Presence, Moses is instructed to prepare for the experience, which actually constitutes the reinstatement of the covenant. This is underscored by several points of contact between this narrative and the account of the original theophany at Sinai: The shattered tablets, which once testified to the reality of the covenant, are to be replaced; the original text incised upon them is to be reproduced; Moses is told to "be ready" by morning just as the people had been ordered to "be ready" for the occasion (19:11,15); access CO the mountain is severely restricted in both experiences (34:3; 19:12-13); the Lord again "comes down" upon Sinai (34:5; 19:9,18,20); and the event evokes such fear that the people back away (34:30; 20:15-18).
quotes.speaker: Nahum Sarna
quotes.attr: Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 215

quotes.text: The Israelites were distinguished in the first place as worshipers of a living God, a personal God, in whose image they had been created and made. [Gen. 1:26-27; Ex. 33:21-23.] No other nation on the face of the earth recognized the living God. That was a sign by which the covenant people, descendants of Abraham, through Isaac and Jacob, were known. . . . Are we worshipping the true and living God, or are we going idolatrously after the gods of gold and silver, of iron and wood, and brass, diamonds, and other idols of wealth? Are we worshipping our farms, our cattle and sheep? Who is our God?
quotes.speaker: James E. Talmage
quotes.attr: James E. Talmage, CR, October 1930, pp. 71, 73
